hints: A programming technique where a solution is built by solving smaller versions of the same problem
Every use of this technique requires a base case — otherwise it runs forever
Classic example: computing a factorial or traversing a tree
The call stack grows with each invocation — too many levels causes a stack overflow
Defined formally as: a function that calls itself with modified arguments until reaching the base case
A defined contract that lets two pieces of software talk to each other
You use one every time you fetch weather data or log in with Google
REST and GraphQL are two common styles for building these on the web
Defines endpoints, request formats, and response schemas — but hides internal implementation
Stands for Application Programming Interface
A fast way to find an item in a sorted list
Cuts the search space in half with every comparison
Requires the data to be sorted — otherwise it gives wrong answers
Time complexity: O(log n) — far better than scanning every element
Works by comparing the target to the middle element and eliminating half the range
A temporary store of data that makes future access faster
Your browser uses one to avoid re-downloading images every visit
CPUs have multiple layers of it (L1, L2, L3) sitting between the processor and RAM
The fundamental tradeoff: faster access, but data can go stale
A cache miss means the data wasn't found and must be fetched from the slower source
A situation where a system can make no further progress
Occurs in concurrent programs when multiple threads wait on each other indefinitely
Like two people each holding a door for the other — neither can move
Requires four conditions: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
Classic example: thread A holds lock 1 and waits for lock 2, while thread B holds lock 2 and waits for lock 1
The process of scrambling data so only authorized parties can read it
Used every time you visit an HTTPS website
Symmetric vs asymmetric are its two main categories
AES and RSA are two of the most widely deployed algorithms
Converts plaintext into ciphertext using a key — decryption reverses the process with the same or paired key
A data structure that maps keys to values for extremely fast lookups
Used under the hood in Python dicts and JavaScript objects
A hash function determines where each key is stored
Average-case lookup, insert, and delete are all O(1)
Collisions — two keys mapping to the same slot — are handled by chaining or open addressing
The core component of an operating system
Manages communication between hardware and software
Linux is named after it — Linus Torvalds wrote the first version in 1991
Handles memory management, process scheduling, and device drivers
User programs can't access hardware directly — they make system calls to it
The standard language for interacting with relational databases
Used by MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite, and most major databases
SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE are its four core operations
Declarative — you describe what you want, not how to get it
Stands for Structured Query Language, though it's pronounced 'sequel' by many
A foundational protocol of the internet
Unlike its sibling UDP, it guarantees delivery and ordering of packets
Used by HTTP, email, and file transfers where data integrity matters
Establishes connections with a 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK)
Stands for Transmission Control Protocol
An automatic memory management technique
Frees programmers from manually tracking and releasing memory
Used in Java, Python, Go, and many modern languages
Can cause 'stop-the-world' pauses that briefly freeze a running program
Finds objects that are no longer reachable from any active reference and reclaims their memory
Software that emulates a complete computer inside another computer
Lets you run Linux on a Windows machine without rebooting
Cloud providers like AWS and Azure sell access to thousands of these
Isolated from the host — a crash inside it doesn't affect the outer system
Managed by a hypervisor (VMware, VirtualBox, KVM) that allocates host hardware resources
A linear data structure where elements are stored in nodes
Unlike an array, elements are not stored contiguously in memory
Each node holds a value and a pointer to the next node
Insertion and deletion at the head are O(1) — but random access is O(n)
A doubly linked list also has a pointer pointing backward to the previous node
A program that translates source code into another form
Turns human-readable code into machine instructions before the program runs
GCC, Clang, and javac are well-known examples
Performs lexing, parsing, optimization, and code generation as separate stages
Contrast with an interpreter, which executes source code line by line at runtime
The delay between a request being sent and a response arriving
Gamers know it as 'ping' — high values mean a laggy experience
Measured in milliseconds for network communication
Affected by physical distance, number of hops, and congestion on the network
Distinct from throughput — a pipe can carry a lot of water (high throughput) but still take time to start flowing (latency)
A data structure that operates on a last-in, first-out principle
Think of a stack of plates — you always take from the top
Your browser's back button uses one to track navigation history
Supports two primary operations: push (add to top) and pop (remove from top)
Also refers to the call stack — the record of active function calls in a running program
A reference that points to nothing
Dereferencing one is one of the most common runtime crashes in programming
Tony Hoare called inventing it his 'billion-dollar mistake'
In Java, accessing a field on one throws a NullPointerException
Modern languages like Rust and Kotlin eliminate it with Option/Maybe types or nullable distinctions
The phonebook of the internet
Translates human-readable names into IP addresses
When you type google.com, this system figures out the actual address to connect to
Hierarchical: root servers → TLD servers (.com, .org) → authoritative servers for each domain
Stands for Domain Name System
A mathematical notation describing how an algorithm's cost scales with input size
O(1) is constant time, O(n) is linear, O(n²) gets bad fast
Ignores constants — O(2n) and O(n) are considered the same
Used to compare algorithms without running them on specific hardware
The 'O' comes from 'order of' — formally it describes an upper bound on growth rate
The most widely used version control system in the world
Tracks changes to files over time so you can revert, branch, and merge
Created by Linus Torvalds in 2005 to manage Linux kernel development
Distributed — every developer has a full copy of the history locally
GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket are hosting platforms built around it
Dealing with multiple things happening at overlapping time intervals
Not the same as parallelism — things can be concurrent on a single CPU core
Web servers use this to handle thousands of requests without dedicated threads per user
Introduces complexity: race conditions, deadlocks, and synchronization problems
Go's goroutines and JavaScript's event loop are two very different models for achieving it
The foundation of data communication on the World Wide Web
Every time you visit a website, your browser uses this
Stateless — each request is independent, which is why cookies exist
Methods include GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, and PATCH
Stands for HyperText Transfer Protocol; the secure version adds TLS and becomes HTTPS
A platform for packaging and running applications in isolated environments
Solves the classic 'it works on my machine' problem
Lighter than a virtual machine — shares the host OS kernel
Apps run in containers built from images defined in a Dockerfile
The standard tool for containerization; Kubernetes is often used to orchestrate many of them
A procedure for arranging elements in a defined order
One of the most studied categories in computer science
QuickSort and MergeSort are the workhorses of most standard libraries
Bubble Sort is famous for being simple and famously slow — O(n²)
The theoretical lower bound for comparison-based versions is O(n log n)
The smallest unit of execution that can be scheduled by an operating system
Multiple ones can share memory within the same process
Creating too many causes overhead — the OS spends more time switching between them than doing work
Synchronization primitives like mutexes and semaphores coordinate access to shared data
Contrast with a process, which has its own separate memory space
A numerical label assigned to each device on a network
Your router has one visible to the internet, and each device on your home network has a private one
IPv4 uses 32 bits — the format 192.168.1.1 is familiar to anyone who's configured a router
The internet is slowly migrating to IPv6 because IPv4 addresses are exhausted
Stands for Internet Protocol address — it's what DNS resolves domain names to
The process of identifying and fixing errors in software
The term originates from a 1947 incident where an actual moth caused a relay failure in a Harvard computer
Tools like breakpoints, step-through execution, and watch variables are core techniques
printf/console.log debugging is widely mocked but widely used
A debugger lets you pause a running program and inspect memory, variables, and the call stack at any point
The maximum rate at which data can be transferred over a network connection
Often confused with latency — high bandwidth doesn't mean low delay
Measured in bits per second (Mbps, Gbps)
Streaming 4K video requires roughly 25 Mbps
Like a highway's number of lanes — more lanes move more cars simultaneously, but don't reduce the travel time
A data type with only two possible values
Named after mathematician George Boole
The foundation of all conditional logic in programming
In most languages: true or false, 1 or 0
Boolean algebra — AND, OR, NOT operations — is the mathematical basis of digital circuit design
Hiding complex implementation details behind a simpler interface
One of the four pillars of object-oriented programming
You use it every time you call a function without knowing how it's implemented
Operating systems provide it — you call 'open file' without knowing the disk sectors involved
Reduces cognitive load by letting programmers work at a higher level without worrying about lower-level mechanics
